title: V
html:
V

Four-and-twenty hours before this time Sue had written the following note to Jude:

It is as I told you; and I am leaving tomorrow evening. Richard and I thought it could be done with less obtrusiveness after dark. I feel rather frightened, and therefore ask you to be sure you are on the Melchester platform to meet me. I arrive at a little to seven. I know you will, of course, dear Jude; but I feel so timid that I can‚Äôt help begging you to be punctual. He has been so very kind to me through it all!

Now to our meeting! S.

As she was carried by the omnibus further and further down from the mountain townÔªø‚Äîthe single passenger that eveningÔªø‚Äîshe regarded the receding road with a sad face. But no hesitation was apparent therein.

The up-train by which she was departing stopped by signal only. To Sue it seemed strange that such a powerful organization as a railway-train should be brought to a standstill on purpose for herÔªø‚Äîa fugitive from her lawful home.

The twenty minutes‚Äô journey drew towards its close, and Sue began gathering her things together to alight. At the moment that the train came to a standstill by the Melchester platform a hand was laid on the door and she beheld Jude. He entered the compartment promptly. He had a black bag in his hand, and was dressed in the dark suit he wore on Sundays and in the evening after work. Altogether he looked a very handsome young fellow, his ardent affection for her burning in his eyes.

‚ÄúO Jude!‚Äù She clasped his hand with both hers, and her tense state caused her to simmer over in a little succession of dry sobs. ‚ÄúIÔªø‚ÄîI am so glad! I get out here?‚Äù

‚ÄúNo. I get in, dear one! I‚Äôve packed. Besides this bag I‚Äôve only a big box which is labelled.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut don‚Äôt I get out? Aren‚Äôt we going to stay here?‚Äù

‚ÄúWe couldn‚Äôt possibly, don‚Äôt you see. We are known hereÔªø‚ÄîI, at any rate, am well known. I‚Äôve booked for Aldbrickham; and here‚Äôs your ticket for the same place, as you have only one to here.‚Äù

‚ÄúI thought we should have stayed here,‚Äù she repeated.

‚ÄúIt wouldn‚Äôt have done at all.‚Äù

‚ÄúAh!Ôªø‚ÄîPerhaps not.‚Äù

‚ÄúThere wasn‚Äôt time for me to write and say the place I had decided on. Aldbrickham is a much bigger townÔªø‚Äîsixty or seventy thousand inhabitantsÔªø‚Äîand nobody knows anything about us there.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd you have given up your Cathedral work here?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes. It was rather suddenÔªø‚Äîyour message coming unexpectedly. Strictly, I might have been made to finish out the week. But I pleaded urgency and I was let off. I would have deserted any day at your command, dear Sue. I have deserted more than that for you!‚Äù

‚ÄúI fear I am doing you a lot of harm. Ruining your prospects of the Church; ruining your progress in your trade; everything!‚Äù

‚ÄúThe Church is no more to me. Let it lie! I am not to be one of

‚ÄòThe soldier-saints who, row on row,
Burn upward each to his point of bliss,‚Äô

if any such there be! My point of bliss is not upward, but here.‚Äù

‚ÄúO I seem so badÔªø‚Äîupsetting men‚Äôs courses like this!‚Äù said she, taking up in her voice the emotion that had begun in his. But she recovered her equanimity by the time they had travelled a dozen miles.

‚ÄúHe has been so good in letting me go,‚Äù she resumed. ‚ÄúAnd here‚Äôs a note I found on my dressing-table, addressed to you.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes. He‚Äôs not an unworthy fellow,‚Äù said Jude, glancing at the note. ‚ÄúAnd I am ashamed of myself for hating him because he married you.‚Äù

‚ÄúAccording to the rule of women‚Äôs whims I suppose I ought to suddenly love him, because he has let me go so generously and unexpectedly,‚Äù she answered smiling. ‚ÄúBut I am so cold, or devoid of gratitude, or so something, that even this generosity hasn‚Äôt made me love him, or repent, or want to stay with him as his wife; although I do feel I like his large-mindedness, and respect him more than ever.‚Äù

‚ÄúIt may not work so well for us as if he had been less kind, and you had run away against his will,‚Äù murmured Jude.

‚ÄúThat I never would have done.‚Äù

Jude‚Äôs eyes rested musingly on her face. Then he suddenly kissed her; and was going to kiss her again. ‚ÄúNoÔªø‚Äîonly once nowÔªø‚Äîplease, Jude!‚Äù

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs rather cruel,‚Äù he answered; but acquiesced. ‚ÄúSuch a strange thing has happened to me,‚Äù Jude continued after a silence. ‚ÄúArabella has actually written to ask me to get a divorce from herÔªø‚Äîin kindness to her, she says. She wants to honestly and legally marry that man she has already married virtually; and begs me to enable her to do it.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhat have you done?‚Äù

‚ÄúI have agreed. I thought at first I couldn‚Äôt do it without getting her into trouble about that second marriage, and I don‚Äôt want to injure her in any way. Perhaps she‚Äôs no worse than I am, after all! But nobody knows about it over here, and I find it will not be a difficult proceeding at all. If she wants to start afresh I have only too obvious reasons for not hindering her.‚Äù

‚ÄúThen you‚Äôll be free?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, I shall be free.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhere are we booked for?‚Äù she asked, with the discontinuity that marked her tonight.

‚ÄúAldbrickham, as I said.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut it will be very late when we get there?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes. I thought of that, and I wired for a room for us at the Temperance Hotel there.‚Äù

‚ÄúOne?‚Äù

‚ÄúYesÔªø‚Äîone.‚Äù

She looked at him. ‚ÄúO Jude!‚Äù Sue bent her forehead against the corner of the compartment. ‚ÄúI thought you might do it; and that I was deceiving you. But I didn‚Äôt mean that!‚Äù

In the pause which followed, Jude‚Äôs eyes fixed themselves with a stultified expression on the opposite seat. ‚ÄúWell!‚Äù he said.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ ‚ÄúWell!‚Äù

He remained in silence; and seeing how discomfited he was she put her face against his cheek, murmuring, ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt be vexed, dear!‚Äù

‚ÄúOhÔªø‚Äîthere‚Äôs no harm done,‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúButÔªø‚ÄîI understood it like that.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ Is this a sudden change of mind?‚Äù

‚ÄúYou have no right to ask me such a question; and I shan‚Äôt answer!‚Äù she said, smiling.

‚ÄúMy dear one, your happiness is more to me than anythingÔªø‚Äîalthough we seem to verge on quarrelling so often!Ôªø‚Äîand your will is law to me. I am something more than a mereÔªø‚Äîselfish fellow, I hope. Have it as you wish!‚Äù On reflection his brow showed perplexity. ‚ÄúBut perhaps it is that you don‚Äôt love meÔªø‚Äînot that you have become conventional! Much as, under your teaching, I hate convention, I hope it is that, not the other terrible alternative!‚Äù

Even at this obvious moment for candour Sue could not be quite candid as to the state of that mystery, her heart. ‚ÄúPut it down to my timidity,‚Äù she said with hurried evasiveness; ‚Äúto a woman‚Äôs natural timidity when the crisis comes. I may feel as well as you that I have a perfect right to live with you as you thoughtÔªø‚Äîfrom this moment. I may hold the opinion that, in a proper state of society, the father of a woman‚Äôs child will be as much a private matter of hers as the cut of her under-linen, on whom nobody will have any right to question her. But partly, perhaps, because it is by his generosity that I am now free, I would rather not be other than a little rigid. If there had been a rope-ladder, and he had run after us with pistols, it would have seemed different, and I may have acted otherwise. But don‚Äôt press me and criticize me, Jude! Assume that I haven‚Äôt the courage of my opinions. I know I am a poor miserable creature. My nature is not so passionate as yours!‚Äù

He repeated simply: ‚ÄúI thoughtÔªø‚Äîwhat I naturally thought. But if we are not lovers, we are not. Phillotson thought so, I am sure. See, here is what he has written to me.‚Äù He opened the letter she had brought, and read:

‚ÄúI make only one conditionÔªø‚Äîthat you are tender and kind to her. I know you love her. But even love may be cruel at times. You are made for each other: it is obvious, palpable, to any unbiased older person. You were all along ‚Äòthe shadowy third‚Äô in my short life with her. I repeat, take care of Sue.‚Äù

‚ÄúHe‚Äôs a good fellow, isn‚Äôt he!‚Äù she said with latent tears. On reconsideration she added, ‚ÄúHe was very resigned to letting me goÔªø‚Äîtoo resigned almost! I never was so near being in love with him as when he made such thoughtful arrangements for my being comfortable on my journey, and offering to provide money. Yet I was not. If I loved him ever so little as a wife, I‚Äôd go back to him even now.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut you don‚Äôt, do you?‚Äù

‚ÄúIt is trueÔªø‚ÄîO so terribly true!Ôªø‚ÄîI don‚Äôt.‚Äù

‚ÄúNor me neither, I half fear!‚Äù he said pettishly. ‚ÄúNor anybody perhaps!Ôªø‚ÄîSue, sometimes, when I am vexed with you, I think you are incapable of real love.‚Äù

‚ÄúThat‚Äôs not good and loyal of you!‚Äù she said, and drawing away from him as far as she could, looked severely out into the darkness. She added in hurt tones, without turning round: ‚ÄúMy liking for you is not as some women‚Äôs perhaps. But it is a delight in being with you, of a supremely delicate kind, and I don‚Äôt want to go further and risk it byÔªø‚Äîan attempt to intensify it! I quite realized that, as woman with man, it was a risk to come. But, as me with you, I resolved to trust you to set my wishes above your gratification. Don‚Äôt discuss it further, dear Jude!‚Äù

‚ÄúOf course, if it would make you reproach yourselfÔªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ but you do like me very much, Sue? say you do! Say that you do a quarter, a tenth, as much as I do you; and I‚Äôll be content!‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôve let you kiss me, and that tells enough.‚Äù

‚ÄúJust once or so!‚Äù

‚ÄúWellÔªø‚Äîdon‚Äôt be a greedy boy.‚Äù

He leant back, and did not look at her for a long time. That episode in her past history of which she had told himÔªø‚Äîof the poor Christminster graduate whom she had handled thus, returned to Jude‚Äôs mind; and he saw himself as a possible second in such a torturing destiny.

‚ÄúThis is a queer elopement!‚Äù he murmured. ‚ÄúPerhaps you are making a cat‚Äôs-paw of me with Phillotson all this time. Upon my word it almost seems soÔªø‚Äîto see you sitting up there so prim!‚Äù

‚ÄúNow you mustn‚Äôt be angryÔªø‚ÄîI won‚Äôt let you!‚Äù she coaxed, turning and moving nearer to him. ‚ÄúYou did kiss me just now, you know; and I didn‚Äôt dislike you to, I own it, Jude. Only I don‚Äôt want to let you do it again, just yetÔªø‚Äîconsidering how we are circumstanced, don‚Äôt you see!‚Äù

He could never resist her when she pleaded (as she well knew). And they sat side by side with joined hands, till she aroused herself at some thought.

‚ÄúI can‚Äôt possibly go to that Temperance Inn, after your telegraphing that message!‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy not?‚Äù

‚ÄúYou can see well enough!‚Äù

‚ÄúVery well; there‚Äôll be some other one open, no doubt. I have sometimes thought, since your marrying Phillotson because of a stupid scandal, that under the affectation of independent views you are as enslaved to the social code as any woman I know!‚Äù

‚ÄúNot mentally. But I haven‚Äôt the courage of my views, as I said before. I didn‚Äôt marry him altogether because of the scandal. But sometimes a woman‚Äôs love of being loved gets the better of her conscience, and though she is agonized at the thought of treating a man cruelly, she encourages him to love her while she doesn‚Äôt love him at all. Then, when she sees him suffering, her remorse sets in, and she does what she can to repair the wrong.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou simply mean that you flirted outrageously with him, poor old chap, and then repented, and to make reparation, married him, though you tortured yourself to death by doing it.‚Äù

‚ÄúWellÔªø‚Äîif you will put it brutally!Ôªø‚Äîit was a little like thatÔªø‚Äîthat and the scandal togetherÔªø‚Äîand your concealing from me what you ought to have told me before!‚Äù

He could see that she was distressed and tearful at his criticisms, and soothed her, saying: ‚ÄúThere, dear; don‚Äôt mind! Crucify me, if you will! You know you are all the world to me, whatever you do!‚Äù

‚ÄúI am very bad and unprincipledÔªø‚ÄîI know you think that!‚Äù she said, trying to blink away her tears.

‚ÄúI think and know you are my dear Sue, from whom neither length nor breadth, nor things present nor things to come, can divide me!‚Äù

Though so sophisticated in many things she was such a child in others that this satisfied her, and they reached the end of their journey on the best of terms. It was about ten o‚Äôclock when they arrived at Aldbrickham, the county town of North Wessex. As she would not go to the Temperance Hotel because of the form of his telegram, Jude inquired for another; and a youth who volunteered to find one wheeled their luggage to The George further on, which proved to be the inn at which Jude had stayed with Arabella on that one occasion of their meeting after their division for years.

Owing, however, to their now entering it by another door, and to his preoccupation, he did not at first recognize the place. When they had engaged their respective rooms they went down to a late supper. During Jude‚Äôs temporary absence the waiting-maid spoke to Sue.

‚ÄúI think, ma‚Äôam, I remember your relation, or friend, or whatever he is, coming here once beforeÔªø‚Äîlate, just like this, with his wifeÔªø‚Äîa lady, at any rate, that wasn‚Äôt you by no manner of meansÔªø‚Äîjest as med be with you now.‚Äù

‚ÄúO do you?‚Äù said Sue, with a certain sickness of heart. ‚ÄúThough I think you must be mistaken! How long ago was it?‚Äù

‚ÄúAbout a month or two. A handsome, full-figured woman. They had this room.‚Äù

When Jude came back and sat down to supper Sue seemed moping and miserable. ‚ÄúJude,‚Äù she said to him plaintively, at their parting that night upon the landing, ‚Äúit is not so nice and pleasant as it used to be with us! I don‚Äôt like it hereÔªø‚ÄîI can‚Äôt bear the place! And I don‚Äôt like you so well as I did!‚Äù

‚ÄúHow fidgeted you seem, dear! Why do you change like this?‚Äù

‚ÄúBecause it was cruel to bring me here!‚Äù

‚ÄúWhy?‚Äù

‚ÄúYou were lately here with Arabella. There, now I have said it!‚Äù

‚ÄúDear me, whyÔªø‚Äî‚Äù said Jude looking round him. ‚ÄúYesÔªø‚Äîit is the same! I really didn‚Äôt know it, Sue. WellÔªø‚Äîit is not cruel, since we have come as we haveÔªø‚Äîtwo relations staying together.‚Äù

‚ÄúHow long ago was it you were here? Tell me, tell me!‚Äù

‚ÄúThe day before I met you in Christminster, when we went back to Marygreen together. I told you I had met her.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, you said you had met her, but you didn‚Äôt tell me all. Your story was that you had met as estranged people, who were not husband and wife at all in Heaven‚Äôs sightÔªø‚Äînot that you had made it up with her.‚Äù

‚ÄúWe didn‚Äôt make it up,‚Äù he said sadly. ‚ÄúI can‚Äôt explain, Sue.‚Äù

‚ÄúYou‚Äôve been false to me; you, my last hope! And I shall never forget it, never!‚Äù

‚ÄúBut by your own wish, dear Sue, we are only to be friends, not lovers! It is so very inconsistent of you toÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúFriends can be jealous!‚Äù

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt see that. You concede nothing to me and I have to concede everything to you. After all, you were on good terms with your husband at that time.‚Äù

‚ÄúNo, I wasn‚Äôt, Jude. O how can you think so! And you have taken me in, even if you didn‚Äôt intend to.‚Äù She was so mortified that he was obliged to take her into her room and close the door lest the people should hear. ‚ÄúWas it this room? Yes it wasÔªø‚ÄîI see by your look it was! I won‚Äôt have it for mine! O it was treacherous of you to have her again! I jumped out of the window!‚Äù

‚ÄúBut Sue, she was, after all, my legal wife, if notÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

Slipping down on her knees Sue buried her face in the bed and wept.

‚ÄúI never knew such an unreasonableÔªø‚Äîsuch a dog-in-the-manger feeling,‚Äù said Jude. ‚ÄúI am not to approach you, nor anybody else!‚Äù

‚ÄúO don‚Äôt you understand my feeling! Why don‚Äôt you! Why are you so gross! I jumped out of the window!‚Äù

‚ÄúJumped out of window?‚Äù

‚ÄúI can‚Äôt explain!‚Äù

It was true that he did not understand her feeling very well. But he did a little; and began to love her none the less.

‚ÄúIÔªø‚ÄîI thought you cared for nobodyÔªø‚Äîdesired nobody in the world but me at that timeÔªø‚Äîand ever since!‚Äù continued Sue.

‚ÄúIt is true. I did not, and don‚Äôt now!‚Äù said Jude, as distressed as she.

‚ÄúBut you must have thought much of her! OrÔªø‚Äî‚Äù

‚ÄúNoÔªø‚ÄîI need notÔªø‚Äîyou don‚Äôt understand me eitherÔªø‚Äîwomen never do! Why should you get into such a tantrum about nothing?‚Äù

Looking up from the quilt she pouted provokingly: ‚ÄúIf it hadn‚Äôt been for that, perhaps I would have gone on to the Temperance Hotel, after all, as you proposed; for I was beginning to think I did belong to you!‚Äù

‚ÄúO, it is of no consequence!‚Äù said Jude distantly.

‚ÄúI thought, of course, that she had never been really your wife since she left you of her own accord years and years ago! My sense of it was, that a parting such as yours from her, and mine from him, ended the marriage.‚Äù

‚ÄúI can‚Äôt say more without speaking against her, and I don‚Äôt want to do that,‚Äù said he. ‚ÄúYet I must tell you one thing, which would settle the matter in any case. She has married another manÔªø‚Äîreally married him! I knew nothing about it till after the visit we made here.‚Äù

‚ÄúMarried another?Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶ It is a crimeÔªø‚Äîas the world treats it, but does not believe.‚Äù

‚ÄúThereÔªø‚Äînow you are yourself again. Yes, it is a crimeÔªø‚Äîas you don‚Äôt hold, but would fearfully concede. But I shall never inform against her! and it is evidently a prick of conscience in her that has led her to urge me to get a divorce, that she may remarry this man legally. So you perceive I shall not be likely to see her again.‚Äù

‚ÄúAnd you didn‚Äôt really know anything of this when you saw her?‚Äù said Sue more gently, as she rose.

‚ÄúI did not. Considering all things, I don‚Äôt think you ought to be angry, darling!‚Äù

‚ÄúI am not. But I shan‚Äôt go to the Temperance Hotel!‚Äù

He laughed. ‚ÄúNever mind!‚Äù he said. ‚ÄúSo that I am near you, I am comparatively happy. It is more than this earthly wretch called Me deservesÔªø‚Äîyou spirit, you disembodied creature, you dear, sweet, tantalizing phantomÔªø‚Äîhardly flesh at all; so that when I put my arms round you I almost expect them to pass through you as through air! Forgive me for being gross, as you call it! Remember that our calling cousins when really strangers was a snare. The enmity of our parents gave a piquancy to you in my eyes that was intenser even than the novelty of ordinary new acquaintance.‚Äù

‚ÄúSay those pretty lines, then, from Shelley‚Äôs ‚ÄòEpipsychidion‚Äô as if they meant me!‚Äù she solicited, slanting up closer to him as they stood. ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt you know them?‚Äù

‚ÄúI know hardly any poetry,‚Äù he replied mournfully.

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt you? These are some of them:

‚ÄòThere was a Being whom my spirit oft
Met on its visioned wanderings far aloft.
‚ãÆ
A seraph of Heaven, too gentle to be human,
Veiling beneath that radiant form of woman.Ôªø‚ÄäÔªø‚Ä¶‚Äô

O it is too flattering, so I won‚Äôt go on! But say it‚Äôs me!Ôªø‚Äîsay it‚Äôs me!‚Äù

‚ÄúIt is you, dear; exactly like you!‚Äù

‚ÄúNow I forgive you! And you shall kiss me just once thereÔªø‚Äînot very long.‚Äù She put the tip of her finger gingerly to her cheek; and he did as commanded. ‚ÄúYou do care for me very much, don‚Äôt you, in spite of my notÔªø‚Äîyou know?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes, sweet!‚Äù he said with a sigh; and bade her good night.
